speaker: interviewer
text: --19. And we're interviewing Henry Kreisel. And we left off talking about the village.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This-- this family-- this family, they were fundamentalist Christians, as were the family my sister was in. But they-- and so they used to take me Sundays-- there was a hall-- a hall they went to, a regular hall. And one of them used to get up and speak. Even the person I was with, he spoke there-- spoke there. Got up to some-- you know, and to speak. And it never occurred to me this was a church. And then, because I was raised Orthodox, and I was involved with Jews already, because I belonged to this religious Zionist organization, [INAUDIBLE], and I felt Jewish-- there are people who are religiously inclined and there are people who are not religiously inclined. I was religiously inclined-- had gone to cheder. My parents were religious. We're Orthodox Jews. And so I didn't know it was a church. And at night, they made me say a prayer. Our Father, which art in heaven, hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come-- you know. But so-- but, you know-- so saying a prayer, you know, it wasn't-- didn't mean that much, you know, until I-- then I realized that this was a Christian church they were taking me to. And I refused to go to that church. I was 10 years old, but I was old enough to know what was going on. And I refused to go to church. And I refused to say the prayers. And these were very decent people. And they knew that I was-- that I was right. And then we were still thinking in terms of the parents picking us up, you know, and-- and so on. They didn't-- they didn't have ownership on me. Some people assumed ownership of the children. In the case of my sister's, people she went to, they assumed ownership.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they sort of thought they had adopted her, that they belonged to them. It was part of them. A lot of people who were-- who were looking for adoptions-- and you know this as a social worker-- are not qualified to be adoptive parents because they want to have the kid for their own benefit, to fill a void in their-- in their-- a void in their lives which they feel a child might-- might fill. Sometimes it does and sometimes it doesn't. But they sort of have an idea this kid belongs to me and, you know, it's part of me. And I, you know-- has to-- but the people I was with did not have this attitude. And when they saw that I balked at going to church-- and they understood-- they-- they agreed-- they sort arranged for me to leave. So-- and we parted on good terms. And I maintained a relationship with them by-- by letters, occasional visits, until their deaths-- until-- until their death.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay with this family for?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four months. About four months. Maybe five months. I don't exactly, you know? When you're a kid, you don't keep records, you know? Maybe four-- about maybe four or five months-- and not that long, but long enough to establish relationships with them and their extended family, which lived around in the village, at different farms in the village. The-- my the-- the people I was with, the man, he was a farm worker-- didn't have his own farm. And the wife was just a housewife. She took care of things in the house and of-- my-- took care of me and took care of her husband. In those days, you know, being a-- being a housewife was a big chore. There weren't any laundries-- any automatic laundries. And there weren't-- you couldn't buy finished food-- you know, cooked food, prepared food-- in the-- in the market. It had to be all made yourself. If you made-- if you made butter or cheese, you made it yourself. And that-- in that hamlet, there was no-- there was no water in the house. There was a well outside, and the water had to be gotten from the well outside. There was no toilet in the-- in-- in the house at that point. There was a-- there was an outhouse in the yard. There was no electricity. There were paraffin lamps. And in perhaps the whole village, there were perhaps one or two telephones.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was kind of-- and a lot of things were still horsedrawn. A lot of wagons and things were horsedrawn-- although there were some-- some motorcycles and cars, but they were more of a rarity. That goes back to 1939, which people will think is impossible. But it was so still then. And so a lot of things-- going to school was 4 miles each way, you know? That took about an hour, especially in bad weather. But that was-- I-- I came in September, so there was-- the winter was snow, you know? It was cold and, you know-- and so on. And so that took a while to get to school. And-- but I was happy enough, you know? It's sort of like-- sort of-- and-- and I was still corresponding with my parents. They were sending letters to somebody in-- in-- in Belgium. We knew somebody who arranged for my parents to write to Belgium, to some address. And that person would forward those letters to England, because Belgium wasn't in the war yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, my [INAUDIBLE] I could answer back letters, going to-- to give it to this person. And he would send it to Belgium. From there, they would send it on to my parents. So we were corresponding. I was corresponding with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what sort of things they were writing to you about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall what. What do parents write to a 10-year-old kid, you know? And a-- a eight-year-old kid. I think they said to me, take care of your sister and stay in touch. You know, things are well with us. They [INAUDIBLE] they weren't gonna tell-- tell me what-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, see if you can arrange for us to come-- to come there, because it was impossible. What could I do? You know, talk to somebody about it. But there was nothing because the war had started. And the war started, and-- and there was no way of getting from there to England to begin with, although the England-- the English people, Britain, was not about to let more Jews in. Britain and America had-- had limited amounts of people that-- limited numbers of people they took in. And they weren't prepared to take any more in. They took in 10,000 children, all right? Maybe that was their one big gesture. Beyond that, it was hard for adults to get to England-- adult Jews to get to England, unless they had money and they had their own businesses, or they worked at some [INAUDIBLE] people as household help, things like that. When you split up from the Bannister family, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Bannister family, I went to a place in Ipswich, south of England. It was either a former big [INAUDIBLE] or former big army place, because it held several hundred boys. It housed several hundred boys. And-- and we were like-- like you see-- almost like in-- like you see, like, in the-- the private schools-- you know, in different houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: The place, it was called Barham House. The place was called Barham House. I remember-- while I'm talking to you, I remember it. And-- and-- but it was near a railroad station. It was near-- near Ipswich, which is a big industrial town in the south of England. And it received heavy bombings and-- heavy, heavy bombings. And they were afraid the kids might get killed, you know? So they evacuated us from this Barham House, this several hundred children, to different places throughout England. Now, how long were you at this house [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also several months. Not that long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it all Jewish children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Refugee-- Jewish refugee children. Whether they were all really Jewish technically, whether-- the Germans didn't go according to-- to Halakha. They didn't ask are these-- whether the child was a Jewish mother [INAUDIBLE] Halakha. You had to be a child according to either grandparents, you know? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were either their grandparents Jewish? So they were-- Halakhally, there may be a number of kids there which were not Jewish. But according to Germans, they were Jewish. There was a great deal of intermarriage in Germany prior to World War II, prior to the Nazis-- a phenomenal amount of intermarriage, and a phenomenal amount of intermarriage in Austria, and a great deal of assimilation that those kids-- most of them didn't even see themselves as Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, it's conceivable that if their father or-- or-- or that their grandfather or grandmother were Jewish, and the parents were not, they may have gone to regular churches, you know, and weren't even aware of being Jewish, some of them, you know-- and may even have resented being put into the situation of being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of program happened at Barham House? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Barham House, they had classes-- classes in English-- like school classes in English and in other subjects. So of course, having-- having had the experience of going to this other-- my first place, going to regular school and learning English quite well from the other kids, you know, and from school, I had some-- I had-- I had some knowledge of English already. And they put me to one of the higher classes, where I was with some of the older children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the age range of children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The age range of children from the Kindertransport-- I think anywhere from age three to age 17. And I imagine here at Barham House probably from age seven or eight till age 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember forging relationships with these boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: I made some friends. Yeah, I had some-- I had some friends. And there were some friends. And in fact-- and there was a synagogue there. There were a few of the children who went to the synagogue for services, Friday night Sabbaths. And then these-- a couple of these friends, when they distributed all of us, they were going to go to an-- a hostel. And I'll tell you what a hostel is in a minute. The Orthodox hostel. And they told the-- and I wasn't assigned yet. So they told the chaplain I got assigned to that Orthodox hostel also, which had a-- a great impact on my life until this day. In a hostel-- we-- we call in-- in-- in America a group home. So there was a group home for boys. And they had other hostels and group homes for girls. And this being an Orthodox hostel, we had-- we went to a regular public school in daytime, but we also had several other shearim classes in Jewish subjects. We had-- we had prayers every day-- minyan to daven every day. And we also had different shearim-- one in the morning before going to public school, and maybe a couple of shearim after coming back from public school. So we were quite busy. And we also had a-- got a good Jewish background.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many boys were in this home with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think around 25-- maybe 25 to 30. Around 25, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it located? Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was located in Tylers Green, a small village near the town of High Wycombe, which was a known furniture town. And they produced a lot of furniture there. That's what the town was known for. And-- and in the village, we lived in a house called the Old Vicarage. This used-- next to a graveyard, next to a church, because the vicar used to live in that house. It was a big house. It had many rooms in it. And therefore, it could hold 25, 30 kids, you know, even though there was several of-- of us in each room. It was like a dormitory style, five or six in a room. But even so, they could hold the-- the kids and the staff. And downstairs, there was living rooms, and the dining rooms, and also the areas where we had classes, [? in those room. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was run from a-- a private committee, from the Golders Green Beth Hamedrash, a synagogue in Golders Green, where the rabbi was Rabbi Leo Munk and the secretary was Mrs. Rosenfelder-- a very fine lady-- and other people who put a lot of time and effort and money into this, because all of these things cost money, besides time and effort. And they raised those funds. And I guess that's what put the food on the table, and paid for the staff and the rent for the Old Vicarage, and clothing. Whatever it is, they had to raise those funds to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember around when you arrived there, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left there-- I was there from about April 1940-- April or May 1940 till-- and I left there in 1944, around the middle of 1944, to go to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there during the summer months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- I graduated from the eighth grade. Within that time, there was only compulsory education in England until the age 14. So I graduated in 1943. And about a week later, I got a job in a furniture factory. I had gone to shop-- you know, shop. They had woodwork shop. And I liked it so much, I wanted to work in that area. And one of the things was of the-- of the-- of the Jewish committees was to have the kids learn a trade to be able to be financially self-supporting. So that sort of-- that idea was to go in a trade to be eventually self-supporting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was there for a year. And then it got too much already. I-- I enjoyed the hostel, but after being there that long, it got a little bit too much. And I wanted a change. And I went to London. You mentioned that it had such a powerful impact on you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because what it was-- the Jewish studies, that gave me the-- my-- my philosophical background in life. And my decisions subsequently were based upon my Torah education. And I pursued it. Later on, I pursued it. I'm pursuing it to this day. And-- and when I made value judgment, they were based upon Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what you got from this-- this hostel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- I got from that place, essentially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still corresponding with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When the Germans invaded Belgium and France-- believe it was in '41, middle of '41-- then all that correspondence-- they were already part of-- could not-- it was disrupted. So from that time on, they were never-- we were not able to correspond again, after the invasion of the Germans into Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do know what happened to my parents. In beginning of-- I believe it was February 1942, they were sent to-- with a-- with a-- one of those-- [INAUDIBLE] they-- they-- they kept-- the Germans kept records. Everything, you know, in five copies, you know? The Jewish-- the Jewish-- the-- the Jewish organization, the Jew-- the Kultusgemeinde, the Jewish organization, they were in charge of the transport. In other words, they arranged-- the Germans had Jews working for them, doing the dirty work for them. And they arranged for transport. On Monday, 1,000 or 2,000 leave. You know. It was still by train. And [INAUDIBLE] people had to go to the railroad, and then leave. On Tuesday, another 2,000. Whatever it was or whatever number it was-- 1,000 or 1,500.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on that day, the Kultusgemeinde kept records. And when I wrote after the war to the Kultusgemeinde, they gave me the date of February 1942 that my parents were sent to a town in Poland called Opole. O-P-O-L-E, Opole. And I-- and I don't know whether it was a concentration camp or whether from there, it was near a concentration camp. You know? And they, of course, no doubt were killed in one of the concentration camps. I don't know which one. Were you in touch with your sister at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and I always were in touch. My sister and I-- because we lived a few miles apart. And they let us get together, you know? And as a matter of fact, we got together in a church. They took this kid-- they took-- my-- these people, they took the people to the church. And they took me to the church also. We got together there, you know? And-- and I subsequently always was in touch with my sister. I used to visit her. A couple of times, they took her to visit me. And we're still in touch to this day. We're very close. And my sister's people-- people where she lived with were more dogmatic, especially the-- the woman. And she sort of wanted to make sure this-- this kid belonged to her. Well, no one-- no one-- no one gave kid to her. It was only like foster care. It was not an adoption. None of these people would have-- you know, these people, my sister especially, would not have been suitable as adoptive parents here in America. They were old, first of all. They were about 10, 15 years older than the people I went to. They were old-- maybe in their late 50's or 60's already. Maybe in their late 50's. They would normally not have qualified to get a child into their home. But because of the-- the pressure of the moment, the-- the-- the Jewish committee for the children, their-- their priority was save Jews. They weren't asking are these qualified people, are these not-qualified people. Their-- their goal had to be to put children into homes to save lives. That was a priority goal. So-- but this lady took it upon herself. She wanted to really make my sister into her image, like it would be her daughter. And she really tried to press upon her more the Christian path and everything else. And-- and they weren't that anxious. They were not against my coming at that time, but they weren't doing the-- too many encouragements either. But I guess they had to because of pressure from my people, whom I was with and from neighbors. They had to allow some sort of contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your sister uncomfortable with them? Or she was too young?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was only eight years old, barely-- not quite nine when she got there in '39, you know? So she was just not quite nine. And she was a very shy kid. And I was a more boisterous, outgoing boy, so we're quite different personalities. And so she was shy and reticent. I think she-- must have been quite a big, traumatic shock to her to be away from my parents and-- and to be there with strangers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she didn't know any English. And these people were very-- they wanted to own her. They were sweet to her, and kind of coochie-coochie-coo and all that, you know? And-- and they sort of like treating her like a baby. I guess they wanted a baby, which never had. So they really weren't interested in her growth and development as much as their getting the satisfaction of having a baby in the house when she was eight going on nine. And-- and I don't think it was a very happy experience. Being there was not a happy experience for my sister. But what could she do, you know, other than make the best of it? Whereas I don't think she could have stood up to them and say I don't want to go to church, like I did. And that year and a half, being older, made a big difference. And also, our different personalities. I was like a tough kid. And the sort of, you know-- I wasn't gonna do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and my sister was quite different, shy and reticent, and just went along with-- whatever the program was, she went along with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you decided to leave the hostel, were other boys leaving as well? Or you were the only one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the older boys usually left. In the sense, going to London was a sort of, like, independence, like-- like-- like kids go to college, sort of. The-- it's an independence, sort of, and new adventures, and being on your own. And, you know, when you were in a group home, you had to conform to certain things. And-- and I wanted to do something else, really. After a while, you get enough. It was a very fine place, and I enjoy it-- enjoyed it. Not all the kids enjoyed it. Some people did not enjoy this hostels/group home experience. But I did. And it was a good experience for me, but not for everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took a lot of things sort of like an adventure, of course, eventually thinking my parents will pick us and we'll go to America, you know-- without knowing what was going on. I had no idea what these criminals, Nazis, degenerates were doing with people in Europe. Who could have thought it? Who could have believed it? Certainly not a kid. When did you find out what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out after the war. After the war, I began to find out. There were some Jewish children who were in the-- who came to the hostel in '45 from-- who had been in concentration camps, who were also in the-- 14, 15, 16 years old. And they told us about their experiences. Unbelievable experiences. And that's how I found out that-- what was going on. And also, at some point later on, I wrote to the Jewish Kultusgemeinde at someone's advice. And they sent me back that the parents were sent to Opole, which meant that was the last that any records were kept, and the last I guess-- I guess probably within days-- at the most, within weeks-- they were going up in smoke. And their bodies were made into soap, and maybe the-- the skins into lampshades.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you picked up and moved to London, what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London, I-- I had this [INAUDIBLE] work in the furniture factory in High Wycombe, I worked in another furniture factory. I got a job in another furniture factory. And there was a-- a organization for Jewish boys. And they placed me in a-- in a Jewish home as a-- as a border-- room and board. Room and board in a Jewish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name. But they were nice enough people. And I was religious. And I said, is it a kosher home? The people who told me was like, yes. It's kosher. You know, they're not religious, but-- they're not-- they're not observant, but they're kosher. And we used to eat there. There was another boy also-- also there-- [? there than ?] myself, with these people. And that was a way people at that point made up the difference of what they needed in income. The man was-- was an accountant. He was working. The woman was a housewife. And they needed-- they had bought a house. And I guess they had to pay off their mortgage and other expenses. And by renting out a room, one room, and-- because this boy-- other boy and I shared one room-- by feeding them, they got a little money in. It was like a part-time job for the lady of the house. You know, she was-- she was at home anyway, so she took two more meals, you know, the same time. And that's how they make their ends meet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were eating in like-- it was like a-- in the kitchen, like a dining-- side-- like a dining-- side dining place in the-- in the kitchen or next to the kitchen. And this lady was busy all the time, salting the meat and-- and putting-- and-- and washing it-- you know, like she a real-- almost like a rebbetzin, you know? And then one day, my roommate told me that he was walking up the street and following her-- saw she went in a non-Jewish butcher shop. And he looked through the window and saw her buying meat there. So I said, I can't believe it, you know? There she was, doing all these things and, like, you know, washing and everything. She must've washed several times and salted several times. I can't believe it. [INAUDIBLE] don't believe me. Just ask her. So I asked her, and she said yes. And I don't know how she rationalized it, because in that-- in those days, you had-- everything was rationed with coupons. That-- that fellow gave her extra meat off the ration book, you know? That's why she shopped there. And I guess the price was cheaper for non-kosher food and for meat than for kosher meat. So she justified it. I guess they were sort of like Conservative-type Jews, not Orthodox Jews. And so she made things kosher herself, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I couldn't stay there. So I-- so I had to leave. I stopped eating there immediately. And within a couple of days, I went to another place, another room and board, where the lady was Orthodox and kept kosher. And-- and that week, the other kid was [INAUDIBLE] we never-- we didn't socialize together, but he liked-- and we got on together. We shared a room together. We didn't fight each other or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't think of his name. That was so many years ago. He had his bed. I had my bed. He had his place to put his clothes. I had my place. But we-- we got on together, although he went his way, I went my way. And-- and-- but so when I was leaving, he says-- he called me a religious fanatic just for the kosher thing. Of course, he never had a Jewish education. And he may have come from a family which was not even Jewish oriented where he came-- when he came-- before he came to England.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he saw this guy leaving. He said, we got a good deal going here. We get on well together. It's a nice room. Why do you want to leave? And he called me a religious fanatic. And then [INAUDIBLE] two days later, I went to a place and I-- I went to a place of the-- where the [INAUDIBLE] Israel meeting-- kids were meeting. And I went into the place. It was in the basement in-- of a house. And so I went to a kid. I said, hello, Yossi. He moved away. He says, I'm not talking to you. I said, why? He says, you're a shegetz. I said, Yossi, how did I get to be a shegetz? He said, I heard you went to-- to [INAUDIBLE] [? Akiva ?] camp this summer. So [INAUDIBLE] the same week. And I was kind of an ignorant, innocent kind of kid. So I said to myself, how is it possible within a couple of days to be both a shegetz and a religious fanatic? I didn't understand then what I now know the nature of lashon hara. Do you know what lashon hara means? Lashon hara means thing-- evil things about others, sort of gossip about others. And lashon hara always contains within it an element of [HEBREW], needless hate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I wasn't aware of it at that time. But I'm quite well aware of lashon hara and [HEBREW], so I understand it now, what was going on. And everybody wants to be an insider. And when you're an insider, you must have outsiders. This worked not only for the Nazis, who had to have Jews as outsiders, but also works among Jews to-- in their groups, with insiders of their groups, and have the others as outsiders.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't fit. And so this guy was [INAUDIBLE] I was going to the [? Akiva. ?] That made me a shegetz. And the word shegetz comes from sheketz-- sheketz [HEBREW], the abomination of idol worship. It's not just a nice name, shegetz. And that kid was using it. And fanatic? A religious fanatic for not being willing to eat non-kosher food, treif meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I-- so I don't think that either things were justified. But from the position they were coming from, they-- they-- they felt good about being inside of their particular group and calling people whom they felt were on the outside with these definitions, just like the Germans. And Hitler was very smart in this. He picked on the Jews. And because he picked on the Jews in-- in Europe, which was already-- were filled with Christo-Nazis, where the Christian had this Nazi ideology towards Jews, of being Jew haters in extreme; that Hitler was very able to be able to get people from all parts of Europe to go along with him because essentially, they were fighting Jews, even though themselves, they were subordinated to the Nazis-- that helped Hitler take over those countries. That was his greatest move he ever made politically.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna switch tapes. Sorry. Sorry.
